  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

jobs:
  hygiene:
    name: hygiene
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - run: mise run ci:lint

  docs:
    name: docs
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - run: mise run //docs:build

  variants:
    name: variant / ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.runner }}
    timeout-minutes: 120
    env:
      MISE_ENV: ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-vulkan
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
          - mise_env: linux-x64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - mise_env: linux-x64-vulkan
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-vulkan
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-egl
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: ios-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: ios-simulator-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: windows-x64-vulkan
            runner: windows-2022
          - mise_env: windows-x64-wgl
            runner: windows-2022
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps

      - run: mise run configure
      - name: Build native library
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["ios-arm64-metal", "ios-simulator-arm64-metal"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run build
      - name: Test native library
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-egl", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run test
      - name: Build Java FFM binding
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-ffm:build
      - name: Build Java JNI binding
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-jni:build
      - name: Test .NET binding
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //bindings/dotnet:test
      # linux-arm64: no Kotlin/Native host — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-36871
      # windows: no MSVC target — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/SKIKO-446
      - name: Test Kotlin/Native binding
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //bindings/kotlin-native:build
      - name: Test Rust binding
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //bindings/rust:ci
      - name: Test Swift binding
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //bindings/swift:test
      - name: Test Zig binding
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-egl", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //bindings/zig:ci
      - name: Build LWJGL example
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //examples/lwjgl-map:build
      - name: Test Rust example
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //examples/rust-map:ci
      - name: Build Swift example
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["macos-arm64-metal"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //examples/swift-map:build
      - name: Build Zig example
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //examples/zig-map:build
      - name: Run Zig readback example
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-egl", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //examples/zig-readback:run

  required:
    name: ci-required
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 5
    if: ${{ always() }}
    needs:
      - hygiene
      - docs
      - variants
    steps:
      - name: Fail if required jobs failed
        if: ${{ contains(needs.*.result, 'failure') || contains(needs.*.result, 'cancelled') || contains(needs.*.result, 'skipped') }}
        run: |
          echo '${{ toJSON(needs) }}'
          exit 1

      - run: true

Now let me verify the generated YAML parses as valid YAML. Let me write a quick check using a minimal YAML parser... I don't have one. Let me instead reason: the structure is regular. The single quotes inside are fine since the whole thing is an unquoted YAML scalar (starts with `$`). Actually `${{ ... }}` as a YAML plain scalar — does it need quoting? It contains `[`, `]`, `,`, `'`, `"`, `(`, `)`. YAML plain scalars can contain most chars as long as they don't start with special indicators. `${{` starts with `$` which is fine. The `fromJson('["ios-arm64-metal", "ios-simulator-arm64-metal"]')` contains single quotes and brackets. In a YAML plain (unquoted) scalar, `[` and `{` at the START are forbidden, but mid-scalar they're allowed. Single/double quotes mid-scalar are allowed in plain scalars. So `if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["ios-arm64-metal", "ios-simulator-arm64-metal"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}` as a plain scalar is valid YAML. The original workflow used `if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}` which also has single quotes mid-scalar — same pattern, proven valid. Good.

But wait — there's a subtlety: the `"` double quotes inside the plain scalar. YAML plain scalars can contain `"` freely. And `,` — in flow context `,` is special, but this is a block scalar (after `if: `), not flow context, so `,` is fine. Good.

Let me actually try to validate with a YAML parser. Python doesn't have yaml in stdlib. But maybe I can install pyyaml? No network. Let me try importing it — maybe /opt/venv has it.

Let me check.

Now let me also handle: the `ci.yml` is now generated with a header. I need to add it to dprint excludes. And the manifest — I decided NOT to exclude it but write it dprint-stable. Let me reconsider: the manifest arrays — I wrote long ones multiline (stable under taplo) and short ones inline (stable). Let me double check the Kotlin/Native line: `envs = ["linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan"]`. Length: `envs = ` (7) + `["linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan"]`. The array content: `"linux-x64-egl"` (16) + `, ` (2) + `"linux-x64-vulkan"` (19) + `, ` (2) + `"macos-arm64-metal"` (19) + `, ` (2) + `"macos-arm64-vulkan"` (19) = 79, plus brackets 2 = 81, plus `envs = ` 7 = 88. That's exactly 88. dprint lineWidth is 80. taplo would consider this exceeding 80 → expand to multiline! So my inline Kotlin array (88 chars) would be reformatted by dprint to multiline → drift → CI fail.

Damn. So the Kotlin/Native array at 88 chars exceeds dprint's 80 → must be multiline. Let me make it multiline. Let me recompute all inline arrays against 80-char limit (dprint lineWidth 80):

For an inline array `envs = ["a", "b", ...]`, total length = 7 + 2 + sum(len(quoted) + 2 for each except last has no `, `). Actually: `[` + items joined by `, ` + `]`. 

Let me compute each inline one I wrote and check ≤ 80 (with some margin, say ≤ 78 to be safe):

1. Build native library: `envs = ["ios-arm64-metal", "ios-simulator-arm64-metal"]`
   = 7 + `[` + `"ios-arm64-metal"`(17) + `, `(2) + `"ios-simulator-arm64-metal"`(27) + `]` 
   = 7 + 1 + 17 + 2 + 27 + 1 = 55. ≤80 ✓ inline OK.

2. Kotlin/Native: computed 88 > 80 ✗ → MUST be multiline.

3. Swift binding: `envs = ["macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan"]`
   = 7 + 1 + 19 + 2 + 19 + 1 = 49. ≤80 ✓ inline OK.

4. Swift example: `envs = ["macos-arm64-metal"]` = 7+1+19+1 = 28. ✓ inline OK.

So only Kotlin/Native needs to move to multiline. But wait — does taplo/dprint actually use lineWidth 80 for deciding array wrap? dprint config has `lineWidth: 80`. The toml plugin respects the global lineWidth. taplo's array formatting: it tries to fit within lineWidth. So yes, >80 → multiline. The Kotlin one at 88 → multiline. Let me make it multiline.

But hold on — am I sure taplo wraps at exactly lineWidth and not some plugin-specific default? The dprint toml plugin (taplo-wasm) uses the dprint configuration's lineWidth. I'm fairly confident. To be safe, I'll make Kotlin multiline.

Actually, to be MAXIMALLY safe and avoid all this boundary guessing, let me reconsider excluding the manifest from dprint. The risk with NOT excluding is these boundary cases. I've found one (Kotlin). There might be others I miscounted. 

Let me recompute ALL envs arrays (inline ones) precisely and also recompute whether the long multiline ones might be COLLAPSED by taplo (taplo collapses a multiline array to inline only if it fits within lineWidth). My multiline arrays are all >80 when inline, so taplo keeps them multiline. Good. But the Kotlin one I wrote inline at 88 → taplo expands. And are there any multiline ones I wrote that are actually ≤80 and would be collapsed? Let me check the smallest multiline one: LWJGL `envs = [...]` 4 vulkan items: `["linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan"]` = 1 + 21+2+19+2+21+2+21 + 1 = 90 >80 → multiline correct. The 8-item ones are way over. The 9-item ones way over. So all my multiline choices are correct (all >80). Only Kotlin (88, I wrote inline) is the error. Fix: make Kotlin multiline.

Let me also double-check the `reason` array for Kotlin: 2 long strings. Inline would be huge → multiline correct.

And the `runners` table values are all short inline strings — fine.

So the ONLY fix needed: Kotlin/Native envs → multiline. Let me also reconsider: is there any risk taplo reformats my multiline arrays differently (e.g., different indentation)? taplo multiline array format is exactly:
```

  "a",

]
```
2-space indent (matches dprint indentWidth 2), trailing comma, items one per line. That's what I wrote. Good.

But wait, does taplo use the dprint `indentWidth: 2`? Yes. And trailing comma — taplo adds trailing commas in multiline arrays by default? I believe taplo's default is to keep/add trailing comma in multiline arrays. My multiline arrays have trailing commas. Good. If taplo's default were NO trailing comma, it'd remove mine → drift. Hmm. Let me think... taplo (the dprint plugin) default for `trailing_newline`... actually the relevant taplo option is `array_auto_collapse` and `array_auto_expand` and trailing commas. The dprint taplo plugin by default... I'm not 100% sure about trailing commas.

This boundary uncertainty is exactly why I keep going back and forth. Let me just make a clean decision: **exclude the manifest from dprint**, with a clear comment that it's validated by the generator + tests. This eliminates ALL formatting risk for the manifest and is defensible (the manifest is a validated data file). I'll add it to excludes.

Wait — but actually, hmm, will excluding it look odd? Let me reconsider the cleanest overall: Actually, you know what, the most robust and clean solution given my constraints: keep the manifest formatted (NOT excluded), make Kotlin multiline, and accept that taplo's trailing-comma behavior is the only residual risk. To check taplo's default trailing comma: taplo's default config has `trailing_newline = true` for the file, and for arrays it does NOT force trailing commas by default in older versions but the dprint plugin... 

I really can't verify. Let me just EXCLUDE the manifest. Final. It's the safe, defensible choice. I'll write a comment in dprint.jsonc and the manifest explaining it's generator-validated.

Hmm, but excluding a hand-edited toml from formatting is a smell that a reviewer might push back on. Let me think about what a thoughtful reviewer would prefer...

Actually, the cleanest resolution that avoids BOTH the smell AND the risk: make the manifest formatting-agnostic by keeping it simple, AND verify with the actual dprint. But I can't run dprint.

OK let me make the decision based on reliability: I'll EXCLUDE the generated workflow (clearly correct) and ALSO exclude the manifest, because (a) I cannot verify dprint stability without the tool, (b) the manifest is structurally validated by a tested generator, (c) a CI-red failure from a formatter nit would be worse than the minor smell. I'll document the rationale. A reviewer can remove the exclude and run dprint if they prefer; the generator + tests remain the real gate.

Wait, actually — let me reconsider whether to keep it formatted by checking if maybe pip has a cached yaml/taplo. No. 

Decision: exclude both from dprint, documented. Move on.

The existing repo toml files... do any use multiline arrays? Let me check mise.lock? No that's excluded. pixi.lock excluded. Are there any multiline arrays in committed toml files I can use as a reference for taplo's exact output? Let me search. If I find one, I match it exactly and I'm safe without excluding.

The committed `.mise/config.windows-x64-vulkan.toml` has a multiline array with:
- 2-space indent
- trailing comma

This confirms taplo/dprint's multiline array format. My manifest's multiline arrays match this style exactly. So multiline arrays with trailing comma ARE dprint-stable. 

Now I need to:
1. Make the Kotlin/Native envs array multiline (it's currently inline at 88 chars > 80 lineWidth → taplo would expand it).
2. Keep short arrays inline (they match pixi.toml's inline style → stable).

Let me verify the short inline arrays won't be expanded: they're all < 80 chars. And taplo keeps short inline arrays inline (confirmed by pixi.toml's `platforms = [...]` inline at ~67 chars). The boundary: my Swift binding inline at 49, Build native at 55, Swift example at 28 — all well under 80. Safe.

The Kotlin one at 88 must become multiline. Let me fix it.

Now I'm fairly confident the manifest is dprint-stable WITHOUT excluding it. So I will NOT exclude the manifest — keeping it formatted is cleaner and now I have evidence (the .mise multiline array style) that my format matches. 

But wait — there's still the residual question: would taplo COLLAPSE any of my multiline arrays to inline? taplo collapses a multiline array to inline only if the inline form fits within lineWidth (80). All my multiline arrays are >80 when inline (I verified LWJGL=90, 8-item and 9-item are way over). So none collapse. Good. And the `reason` array (2 long strings) is >80 inline → stays multiline. Good.

So the manifest is dprint-stable as-is EXCEPT the Kotlin inline array. Let me fix that one. Then I do NOT need to exclude the manifest.

For ci.yml (generated), I WILL exclude it from dprint because the long fromJson lines and the generated nature make it appropriate, and I can't be sure pretty_yaml won't reformat it. Actually — wait. Should I check if pretty_yaml would reformat the generated ci.yml? The long `if:` lines... pretty_yaml might try to wrap them? pretty_yaml generally doesn't wrap long scalar values (it can't break a scalar arbitrarily). It might change quote styles or spacing. The `${{ }}` expressions — pretty_yaml leaves them as plain scalars. The main risk: pretty_yaml might reformat the `run: |` block or blank lines.

I'll encode the original conditions as Python functions and assert the generated allow-lists match for all 11 envs.

The behavior-equivalence test is the most valuable. Let me encode the original conditions:

```python

def orig_test_native(e): return not e.startswith("ios-")

def orig_java_jni(e): return orig_java_ffm(e)

def orig_kotlin(e): return orig_java_ffm(e) and not e.startswith("windows-") and not e.startswith("linux-arm64-")
def orig_rust(e): return orig_java_ffm(e)

```

- Use a conftest? No.

from pathlib import Path

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))

part.text:
Good catch by the test. `resolve_envs` raises a KeyError (not ManifestError) when an env is unknown, because `order[env]` fails before validation can report it nicely. I should make `resolve_envs` robust OR validate envs membership before resolving. 

The cleanest fix: in `validate`, check that all envs in a step are known BEFORE calling resolve_envs, OR make resolve_envs tolerant. Since validate calls resolve_envs to get the list then checks `unknown = set(envs) - runner_set`, but resolve_envs already crashed. Let me reorder: validate membership first using the raw envs, then resolve.

Actually, `resolve_envs` is also used by `_render_step` (which assumes validated input). So resolve_envs can assume inputs are valid. The bug is `validate` calls resolve_envs on potentially-invalid input. Let me make validate check raw envs membership directly:

In validate, for each step:
```python
raw = step["envs"]
if raw != "all":
    unknown = sorted(set(raw) - runner_set)
    if unknown:
        raise ManifestError(... unknown variants ...)
envs = resolve_envs(step, all_envs)
if not envs: ...
```

This way unknown envs are caught before resolve_envs. Let me also guard resolve_envs to raise ManifestError for robustness (defensive), since it's a public-ish function. Actually, keeping resolve_envs simple and letting validate pre-check is cleaner. But resolve_envs is also tested directly with valid input. Let me make resolve_envs robust by filtering unknown with a clear error, to avoid surprising KeyError anywhere:

```python
def resolve_envs(step, all_envs):
    raw = step["envs"]
    if raw == "all":
        return list(all_envs)
    order = {env: i for i, env in enumerate(all_envs)}
    unknown = sorted(set(raw) - set(order))
    if unknown:
        raise ManifestError(
            f"step lists unknown variants: {', '.join(unknown)}"
        )
    return sorted(raw, key=lambda env: order[env])
```

But then validate ALSO checks unknown — redundant but harmless. To avoid duplication, let me keep the unknown-check only in validate (before resolve), and keep resolve_envs as-is but ensure validate pre-checks. However, resolve_envs being called in validate with unknown input is the issue. Simplest: pre-check in validate. Let me update validate to check raw envs membership before resolving. And leave resolve_envs simple (it operates on validated data in render path).

Actually, to be safe and DRY, I'll move the unknown check into resolve_envs (raising ManifestError) and remove the now-redundant check in validate. But validate still needs `envs` to check emptiness. Let me restructure validate's step loop:

```python
for index, step in enumerate(steps):
    location = f"step #{index + 1}"
    if "task" not in step:
        raise ManifestError(f"{location} is missing a 'task'")
    task = step["task"]
    if task in seen_tasks:
        raise ManifestError(...)
    seen_tasks.add(task)
    if "envs" not in step:
        raise ManifestError(f"{location} ({task}) is missing 'envs'")
    envs = resolve_envs(step, all_envs)  # raises ManifestError on unknown
    if not envs:
        raise ManifestError(f"{location} ({task}) runs on no variants")
```

And resolve_envs raises ManifestError on unknown envs. This is clean — single source of the unknown-check. The error message from resolve_envs won't include the task name, but that's fine; validate's location context is lost. To keep good messages, I could keep the check in validate with location. Let me keep BOTH the location-aware check in validate is nicer. 

Decision: Keep resolve_envs robust (raise ManifestError on unknown, no KeyError leak) for defensive correctness, AND keep validate's explicit unknown-check with location for better messages. Pre-check in validate happens first, so resolve_envs's check is a backstop. Wait, but if validate pre-checks and raises with location, resolve_envs never sees unknown in validate. And resolve_envs's own check protects the render path / direct calls. Redundant but safe. Actually that's belt-and-suspenders. Let me just do: make resolve_envs robust (ManifestError), and in validate pre-check for location-aware message. Both fine.

Simplest minimal change: make resolve_envs raise ManifestError instead of KeyError. Then the test `test_unknown_env_in_step_is_rejected` expects message "unknown variants" — resolve_envs would raise with "unknown variants" if I phrase it that way. And validate's existing `unknown` check is now redundant but still runs after... no, validate calls resolve_envs FIRST (which raises) before reaching its own unknown check. So the message comes from resolve_envs. Let me make resolve_envs's message match "unknown variants" and include location? It can't easily include location (doesn't know task). 

Cleanest: in validate, do the membership check BEFORE resolve_envs, with location. Then resolve_envs is only called on known-good input in validate. AND make resolve_envs robust anyway (backstop). Let me implement both: reorder validate to check raw envs first.

Let me rewrite the step validation block in validate.

The test caught a real bug: `resolve_envs` raises `KeyError` instead of a clean `ManifestError` for unknown envs. Let me fix both the validator (check membership before resolving, with a location-aware message) and make `resolve_envs` defensive:

part.text:
All 19 tests pass. Now let me wire up the mise tasks and the dprint exclusion.

Let me:
1. Add mise tasks: `ci:generate-workflow`, `ci:check-workflow`, `ci:test-generator`.
2. Call them from `ci:lint`.
3. Exclude `.github/workflows/ci.yml` from dprint.

Let me re-read the current ci:lint task and mise.toml end to add tasks.

Current ci:lint (mise.toml lines 189-198):
```toml
[tasks."ci:lint"]
run = [
  # Warm process-plugin cache before hk runs parallel dprint batches.
  "dprint output-resolved-config > /dev/null",
  "hk fix --all",
  "git update-index -q --refresh",
  # Lockfile drift is common across different mise builds
  "git diff --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
  "git diff --cached --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
]
```

I'll add the workflow sync check and generator tests. But ordering: the `git diff --exit-code` checks run after `hk fix`. If I add `mise run ci:check-workflow` (which regenerates ci.yml) AFTER those diffs, then:
- hk fix runs (dprint formats ci-targets.toml if needed, but excluded ci.yml; ruff formats py files; actionlint checks ci.yml). At this point ci.yml = committed (generated) version.
- git diff checks: catch any drift from hk fix (e.g., if my py/toml wasn't format-stable). ci.yml unchanged (excluded from dprint; actionlint doesn't modify).
- Then ci:check-workflow: regenerate ci.yml → git diff ci.yml. Since committed ci.yml == generator output (verified by test), diff clean.
- Then ci:test-generator: run unit tests.

Wait, but ci:check-workflow regenerates ci.yml in the working tree. After it runs, if I then run more git checks, ci.yml would be the regenerated content. But ci:check-workflow itself does the diff. Order is fine: put ci:check-workflow and ci:test-generator after the existing diffs. But there's a subtlety: ci:check-workflow's `git diff --exit-code -- .github/workflows/ci.yml` compares working-tree (just regenerated) against HEAD. Clean iff committed == generated. Good.

But actually, should the generator tests run before or after the sync check? Order doesn't matter much. Let me put ci:test-generator first (fast, validates generator logic), then ci:check-workflow (sync). Actually, the existing diffs run first. Let me append:
```
"mise run ci:test-generator",
"mise run ci:check-workflow",
```

Hmm, but calling `mise run` inside a mise task's `run` script — does that work? mise tasks can invoke other mise tasks via `mise run <task>` in shell, OR via `depends`. Within a `run` string, `mise run X` is just a shell command that works. The existing ci:lint doesn't do nested mise run, but it's fine. Alternatively use `depends`. But `depends` runs BEFORE `run`. ci:lint needs ordering (fix → refresh → diff). So I can't use depends for the diff ordering. I'll use `mise run` calls in the run list for the new checks, appended after diffs.

Actually, cleaner: make ci:check-workflow and ci:test-generator standalone tasks, and have ci:lint reference them via `mise run`. Let me define them as tasks and append `mise run` lines.

Wait — even simpler: I could fold the generator test and workflow check directly into ci:lint's run list as shell commands (not separate tasks), but then contributors can't run them standalone, and the generate task needs to be standalone anyway. Let me define separate tasks and call them.

Tasks to add:
```toml
[tasks."ci:generate-workflow"]
description = "Regenerate .github/workflows/ci.yml from .github/ci-targets.toml."
run = "python scripts/generate_ci_workflow.py"

[tasks."ci:check-workflow"]
description = "Fail if the checked-in CI workflow is out of sync with its manifest."
hide = true
depends = ["ci:generate-workflow"]
run = "git diff --exit-code -- .github/workflows/ci.yml"

[tasks."ci:test-generator"]
description = "Run the CI workflow generator unit tests."
hide = true
dir = "{{config_root}}/scripts"
run = "python -m unittest test_generate_ci_workflow"
```

Wait, `ci:check-workflow` `depends = ["ci:generate-workflow"]` — depends runs the generate task first (which writes ci.yml), then `run` does the git diff. Good. But `depends` in mise runs dependencies; the generate task writes the file. Then the diff runs. 

But there's a concern: `ci:generate-workflow` writes ci.yml. When `ci:check-workflow` depends on it and runs in ci:lint AFTER hk fix and the broad git diffs, the generate overwrites ci.yml with fresh output. Then `git diff --exit-code -- .github/workflows/ci.yml`. Good.

However, mise `depends` — does it pass args / run in same dir? Default yes. The generate task uses `python scripts/generate_ci_workflow.py` with relative path — needs cwd = repo root. mise file tasks run with cwd = config_root (repo root for root mise.toml). So `python scripts/...` resolves correctly. Good.

For ci:test-generator, I set `dir = "{{config_root}}/scripts"`. Actually for root mise.toml, config_root = repo root. The task runs `python -m unittest test_generate_ci_workflow` with dir=scripts. Good. But wait, `{{config_root}}` for the root config is the repo root. So `dir = "{{config_root}}/scripts"`. Good. Alternatively just `dir = "scripts"`? mise resolves relative dir to config_root. The existing tasks use `dir = "{{env.MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT}}"` and `dir = "{{config_root}}"`. Hmm, some use `{{config_root}}`. For a root-level task, `dir = "scripts"` might resolve relative to cwd, not config_root. To be safe, use `dir = "{{config_root}}/scripts"`. 

Actually wait — is there a subtlety with monorepo config_root? The root mise.toml has `experimental_monorepo_root = true` and config_roots include subdirs. The root config_root is the repo root. `{{config_root}}` in the root mise.toml = repo root. Good.

Now, the python invocation: `python` vs `python3`. mise core:python provides `python`. On the hygiene runner (ubuntu), mise installs core:python 3.14 and puts `python` on PATH. So `python` works. Good. But to be safe cross-platform, mise's python on Windows also provides `python.exe`. Good.

Now, ci:lint additions. Let me append to the run list:
```
"mise run ci:test-generator",
"mise run ci:check-workflow",
```

But hold on — order within ci:lint. The broad `git diff --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'` runs before. At that point, ci.yml is committed version (not yet regenerated), and it's excluded from dprint so hk fix didn't touch it. So that broad diff is clean (assuming my py/toml are format-stable). Then ci:test-generator (no file changes). Then ci:check-workflow (regenerates ci.yml, diffs). Good.

But wait: the broad git diff would ALSO catch if ci.yml was somehow modified by hk fix. Since ci.yml is excluded from dprint, hk fix won't modify it. actionlint (in hk) is check-only (no fix). So ci.yml stays as committed through hk fix. Good.

Now there's another subtle issue: ci:check-workflow regenerates ci.yml and leaves it modified in the working tree (if somehow different). On a clean CI run it's identical so no modification. But if it WERE different, the `git diff --exit-code` in ci:check-workflow catches it (exits 1). Good. The working tree being left modified doesn't matter in CI (ephemeral). Fine.

Now the dprint exclude. Add `".github/workflows/ci.yml"` to dprint.jsonc excludes. Let me also reconsider: should I add a comment in dprint.jsonc explaining? dprint.jsonc is JSONC; comments allowed (it has `//` comments). I'll add a brief inline comment.

Let me look at the current excludes section of dprint.jsonc:
```
"excludes": [
  "third_party/**",
  "**/*-lock.yaml",
  ...
  "**/*.g.cs",
],
```
I'll add `".github/workflows/ci.yml",` with a comment.

Now, let me also reconsider: the manifest `.github/ci-targets.toml` — I'm NOT excluding it. I verified multiline style matches .mise configs. The inline arrays are <80. The Kotlin one is now multiline. I'm confident it's dprint-stable. But let me double-check the `reason` array inline length to be sure it's multiline-stable: 2 strings, each ~85 chars → inline total way over 80 → multiline. I wrote it multiline. Good.

Wait, one more: the header comment block in the manifest has long lines (e.g., "Source of truth for the `variants` job in .github/workflows/ci.yml: which build"). taplo preserves comments as-is (doesn't reflow). Does dprint/taplo reflow comments? taplo does NOT reflow comments by default. So long comment lines are preserved. Good. (If it did reflow, the existing mise.toml comments would be affected too, and they have long lines like the windows_default_file_shell_args comment. So comments are preserved.)

Now let me write the mise.toml additions and dprint exclude.

Let me re-read mise.toml tasks section to insert cleanly.

Actually, I'll add the three new tasks right after the existing `ci:lint` task (or near other ci: tasks). And modify ci:lint's run list.

Let me make the edits.
All 19 tests pass, including the behavior-preservation check. Now let me wire up the mise tasks and exclude the generated workflow from dprint. First the dprint exclusion:

part.text:
The diff shows exactly what I expect: the generated workflow replaces the hand-written `if:` conditions with `contains(fromJson([...]))` allow-lists (behaviorally identical, proven by the test), adds the generated header, and removes the two blank lines that separated the native-test/bindings sections and bindings/examples sections in the original. The blank-line removal is a minor cosmetic change (the generator doesn't emit those intra-section blank lines). That's acceptable — behavior is preserved.

Wait — let me reconsider those blank lines. The original had:
- A blank line between "Test native library" and "Build Java FFM binding" (after the native section).
- A blank line between "Test Zig binding" and "Build LWJGL example" (between bindings and examples).

These were visual section separators. My generator emits all steps contiguously (no blank lines between). This is a minor readability regression in the generated file. Should I preserve section separators?

The manifest groups steps with comments ("# Bindings." and "# Examples." and "# Native library:"). I could emit a blank line + section comment in the workflow for readability. But that adds complexity. Alternatively, emit blank lines before steps that begin a new "section."

Hmm. Actually, the task says "Preserve current CI behavior." Blank lines don't affect behavior. But "campsite rules: leave tidier." Removing visual separators makes the generated file slightly less scannable. However, the generated file now has reason comments (Kotlin) and the section structure is implicit.

I think it's worth preserving readability. Let me add section comments to the workflow generation. I have section markers in the manifest ("# Native library:", "# Bindings.", "# Examples."). But those are free-floating comments in the TOML, not attached to steps. taplo would preserve them but they're not parsed into the step data.

Better approach: add an optional `section` field to the first step of each section, OR emit blank lines between steps based on a `section` key. Let me add a `section` field to steps that start a new section, and render a blank line + comment. Actually simpler: add an optional `comment` field per step that renders as a `# comment` line ABOVE the step (separate from `reason`). But `reason` already renders comments.

Hmm, let me reconsider. The cleanest: keep the manifest's grouping comments, but make them part of the step data so the generator can emit section headers. But TOML comments aren't data.

Option: Add a `section` field. When a step has `section = "Bindings"`, the generator emits a blank line and `      # Bindings` before it. Let me do that — it restores the visual structure AND makes sections explicit in the manifest.

Actually, wait — this adds complexity and the diff is already clean. Let me reconsider whether the blank-line removal matters enough. The generated file is read by humans debugging CI. Section separators help. The original author put them there deliberately. I'll preserve the intent by emitting section comments.

Let me add `section` to the manifest for the first step of each logical group, and render a blank line + section comment. But where exactly did the original blank lines fall?
1. Blank after "Test native library" → before bindings (Java FFM).
2. Blank after "Test Zig binding" → before examples (LWJGL).

So two section breaks: before "Build Java FFM binding" (Bindings) and before "Build LWJGL example" (Examples). The native library steps (configure, build, test) are the first section.

Let me add `section` to:
- "Build Java FFM binding" → section = "Bindings" (emit blank + `# Bindings.`)
- "Build LWJGL example" → section = "Examples" (emit blank + `# Examples.`)

And the manifest's free-floating comments ("# Native library:...", "# Bindings...", "# Examples...") — I'll keep them as manifest documentation but they won't render. OR I'll remove them and rely on `section`. To avoid redundancy, let me convert: remove the free-floating section comments and use `section` fields. But the free-floating comments also document the native-library section and the macOS EGL exclusion rationale. Let me keep the macOS-egl exclusion note as it's useful manifest docs.

Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me simplify: I'll keep the manifest's descriptive comments as-is (they're good manifest documentation), AND add a `section` field to render section separators in the workflow. The `section` field value renders as a comment line preceded by a blank line.

Let me reconsider — is it worth it? The diff currently REMOVES 2 blank lines. If I add section rendering, the generated file gets:
```
      - run: mise run test
      # Bindings
      - name: Build Java FFM binding
```
Wait, original was blank line then step. A `# Bindings` comment is nicer than a bare blank line. But to match original's blank-line-only style... the original just had blank lines, no section comments. Adding `# Bindings` is an improvement (tidier). I'll do blank line + section comment.

Actually, let me reconsider the simplest thing that preserves the visual separation the original had: just emit a blank line before steps that have `section`. No comment needed — matches original exactly (blank line separators). But adding the section name as a comment is strictly more informative. Campsite rule → add the comment.

Let me implement: step can have optional `section` (string). In `_render_step`, before rendering the step, if `section` present, emit a blank line then `      # {section}`.

Wait, but the blank line — in the workflow, between steps there are no blank lines currently in generated output. Adding a blank line before a section step: the `lines` list would have `""` then the comment. Let me handle in render loop:

```python
for step in steps:
    rendered = _render_step(step, all_envs)
    if step.get("section"):
        lines.append("")
        lines.append(f"      # {step['section']}")
    lines.extend(rendered)
```

And remove the section handling from `_render_step`. Let me put it in render's loop.

Hmm, but `_render_step` already prepends `reason` comments. So a section step with reason (none currently) would have section comment then reason comments then the step. Fine.

Let me add `section` to the manifest for the two section starts, and remove the now-redundant free-floating comments? Let me keep the free-floating comments as manifest docs (they explain the macOS-egl exclusion which is valuable). Actually the free-floating "# Bindings." / "# Examples." comments are now redundant with `section`. Let me remove those two and keep the "Native library:" and the macOS-egl explanation ones as manifest prose. Hmm, but "Native library:" is also a section marker. Let me just keep all the manifest comments (they're helpful prose) and ADD section fields. Slight redundancy between "# Bindings." prose comment and `section = "Bindings"` is OK — actually it's a bit redundant.

Let me simplify the manifest: replace the free-floating section comments with `section` fields on the steps. Keep only the substantive prose (the macOS-egl explanation). So:

- Remove "# Native library: iOS variants cross-compile..." → convert to a `section` on... the native section is first, no separator needed before configure. Actually the "Native library" group is the first 3 steps. No blank line before configure (it follows setup-ci-deps with a blank already). So no section marker needed for native. But the prose "iOS variants cross-compile (build only); everything else tests" is useful documentation. Keep it as a comment.
- "# Bindings. macOS EGL is excluded..." → this has two parts: section header + rationale. Split: `section = "Bindings"` on Java FFM step, and keep the macOS-egl rationale as a comment.
- "# Examples." → `section = "Examples"` on LWJGL step.

This is getting fiddly. Let me step back and decide on the cleanest manifest that produces a readable workflow.

Decision: Add optional `section` field. Put it on the first step of Bindings and Examples. Keep the manifest's explanatory comments but trim the pure section-header ones to avoid duplication. Specifically:
- Keep the "Native library" rationale comment (explains build vs test split).
- Replace "# Bindings." line: keep the macOS-egl rationale as a comment, add `section = "Bindings"` to java-ffm.
- Replace "# Examples." line with `section = "Examples"` on lwjgl.

Let me rewrite those parts of the manifest.

Actually, simpler and clean: I'll keep section comments in the manifest as documentation AND emit them via a `section` key, accepting minor redundancy is removed by just using `section`. Let me just do it and view result.

Let me reconsider if this is over-engineering. The task: "Make it declarative, generate the checked-in workflow from manifests, and enforce that it stays in sync. Preserve current CI behavior." The blank-line section separators are cosmetic. Adding `section` is a nice-to-have. Given "campsite rules" and that the original HAD separators, restoring them is the tidier choice. I'll do it but keep it minimal.

Let me update the manifest: add `section` to java-ffm and lwjgl steps; adjust comments.

Let me view the current manifest sections to edit precisely.

The diff is exactly right — behaviorally-identical allow-lists replacing the fragile conditions. But the generator dropped two blank-line section separators the original had (before Bindings, before Examples). Campsite rule: I'll restore that structure explicitly via a `section` field. Let me update the manifest:

metadata.preview:
return lines


def _render_step(step: dict, all_envs: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """Render a single workflow step (reason comment + keys)."""
    envs = resolve_envs(step, all_envs)
    task = step["task"]
    name = step.get("name")
    reason = step.get("reason", [])

    lines = [f"      # {line}" for line in reason]

    fields: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    if name:
        fields.append(("name", name))
    if set(envs) != set(all_envs):
        condition = "${{ " + _contains_condition(envs) + " }}"
        fields.append(("if", condition))
    fields.append(("run", f"mise run {task}"))

display.text:
return lines


def _render_step(step: dict, all_envs: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """Render a single workflow step (reason comment + keys)."""
    envs = resolve_envs(step, all_envs)
    task = step["task"]
    name = step.get("name")
    reason = step.get("reason", [])

    lines = [f"      # {line}" for line in reason]

    fields: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
    if name:
        fields.append(("name", name))
    if set(envs) != set(all_envs):
        condition = "${{ " + _contains_condition(envs) + " }}"
        fields.append(("if", condition))
    fields.append(("run", f"mise run {task}"))

    for index, (key, value) in enumerate(fields):
        prefix = "      - " if index == 0 else "        "
        lines.append(f"{prefix}{key}: {value}")
    return lines

- uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps

      - run: mise run configure
      - name: Build native library
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["ios-arm64-metal", "ios-simulator-arm64-metal"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run build
      - name: Test native library
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-egl", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run test

      # Bindings
      - name: Build Java FFM binding
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-ffm:build
      - name: Build Java JNI binding
        if: ${{ contains(fromJson('["linux-arm64-egl", "linux-arm64-vulkan", "linux-x64-egl", "linux-x64-vulkan", "macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan", "windows-x64-vulkan", "windows-x64-wgl"]'), matrix.mise_env) }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-jni:build
      - name: Test .NET binding

No lines > 88 in either Python file. Good. ruff format uses 88 and won't need to reformat (all lines fit). Let me also double check there are no breakable constructs ruff would change. The main ruff format concerns:
- Trailing commas in multiline collections: I have them.
- Quote style: double quotes throughout.
- Indentation: 4 spaces.

Let me also verify there are no obvious ruff check violations (F-rules):
- Unused imports: in generator I import sys, tomllib, Path — all used. test imports json, os, re, sys, tempfile, unittest, Path, gen — all used? json (json.loads), os (os.path), re (re.search), sys (sys.path), tempfile (tempfile.TemporaryDirectory), unittest, Path (Path(...)), gen. Yes all used.
- F-string without placeholders: none problematic.
- `lambda env: ...` assigned — ruff E731 (lambda assigned to variable) is in E7 (default select)! E731 "do not assign a lambda expression, use a def". My test file uses `ORIGINAL_CONDITIONS = {... "build": lambda env: ..., ...}` — lambdas as dict VALUES, not assigned to a variable name directly. E731 triggers on `name = lambda: ...` assignment. Dict values are not assignments. So `{"build": lambda env: ...}` does NOT trigger E731. Good. Let me double-check: E731 fires for `x = lambda: 0`. For dict/list values it doesn't. So my lambdas in the dict are fine.

Wait, but ruff might still flag? Let me think. E731 is specifically `Assign` nodes where value is Lambda. Dict values are not Assign. So safe.

- The `_not_macos_egl_non_ios` is a def (good, not lambda).

Let me also reconsider: in the test, `step["run"].removeprefix("mise run ")` — removeprefix is 3.9+, fine on 3.12/3.14.

- ruff format: the dict literal `ORIGINAL_CONDITIONS = { ... }` — multi-line dict. ruff format would keep it as I wrote (one key per line, trailing comma). Let me verify my dict has trailing comma after last entry. Looking at my test: the dict ends with `"//examples/zig-readback:run": lambda env: not env.startswith("ios-"),\n}`. Yes trailing comma. Good.

But wait — ruff format might EXPAND or COLLAPSE dict entries. My ORIGINAL_CONDITIONS dict has each key on its own line. ruff format keeps "magic trailing comma" — if there's a trailing comma, it keeps one-per-line. I have trailing comma → stable. Good.

- The `_load()` returns tuple, fine.

- In generator, `fields: list[tuple[str, str]] = []` — fine.

- Generator `validate` — the f-string `f"{location} ({task}) lists unknown variants:" f" {', '.join(unknown)}"` — two adjacent f-strings (implicit concat). ruff format might merge them into one f-string? ruff format (via the `flake8-implicit-str-concat`? no, that's `ISC` not in default). Actually ruff format does NOT merge implicitly-concatenated strings by default? Hmm, actually ruff format DOES merge implicit string concatenation in some cases? Let me think... ruff format leaves implicit string concatenation as-is generally, OR collapses them. I'm not 100% sure. Let me just write it as a single f-string to be safe.

`f"{location} ({task}) lists unknown variants: {', '.join(unknown)}"` — length? location like "step #3", task like "//bindings/dotnet:test", unknown maybe a few. The f-string literal source line: `                raise ManifestError(` then next line `                    f"{location} ({task}) lists unknown variants:"` ... let me check the current source. The concatenation was to keep each line ≤88. A single f-string line:
`                    f"{location} ({task}) lists unknown variants: {', '.join(unknown)}"`
Count indentation (20 spaces) + `f"{location} ({task}) lists unknown variants: {', '.join(unknown)}",`. Let me count the f-string content: `f"..."` ≈ `f"` (2) + `{location}` (9) + ` (` (2) + `{task}` (6) + `) lists unknown variants: ` (25) + `{', '.join(unknown)}` (19) + `"` (1) = 64, + indent 20 + comma 1 = 85. Under 88. So I can make it a single f-string at 85 chars. Safer for ruff (no implicit concat). Let me do that.

Let me look at the current code and fix the two implicit concatenations (the validate one and there might be the concurrency/required ones in render — but those are STRING literals in a list, not f-strings; ruff format leaves list string entries as-is, but might merge implicit-concat adjacent strings? In a list, `"a" "b"` adjacent — ruff format... I think ruff format does NOT merge those either, but to be safe let me reconsider).

Actually, ruff format's behavior on implicit string concatenation: ruff format does NOT collapse or split implicit string concatenation by default. It's left as-is. (The `ISC` rule which would lint it is a separate check rule, not formatting, and not in default select.) So implicit concatenation is format-stable. 

But to be safe and readable, let me simplify the validate f-string to a single line anyway (it fits in 88). The render list entries with implicit concat (concurrency group, required if) — those are long YAML lines that I split for source readability; they're format-stable. #!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Generate ``.github/workflows/ci.yml`` from ``.github/ci-targets.toml``.

The CI target support policy (which build variants run in CI, on which runners,
and which mise task each variant runs) is declared in
``.github/ci-targets.toml``. This module renders that policy into the checked-in
GitHub Actions workflow.

Usage::

    mise run ci:generate-workflow   # write .github/workflows/ci.yml

CI verifies the workflow stays in sync via ``mise run ci:check-workflow``.

The generator cross-checks the manifest against the ``.mise/config.<variant>.toml``
files so a variant cannot exist in CI without a config (or vice versa).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

display.text:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Generate ``.github/workflows/ci.yml`` from ``.github/ci-targets.toml``.

The CI target support policy (which build variants run in CI, on which runners,
and which mise task each variant runs) is declared in
``.github/ci-targets.toml``. This module renders that policy into the checked-in
GitHub Actions workflow.

Usage::

    mise run ci:generate-workflow   # write .github/workflows/ci.yml

CI verifies the workflow stays in sync via ``mise run ci:check-workflow``.

The generator cross-checks the manifest against the ``.mise/config.<variant>.toml``
files so a variant cannot exist in CI without a config (or vice versa).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import sys
import tomllib
from pathlib import Path

REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
MANIFEST_PATH = REPO_ROOT / ".github" / "ci-targets.toml"
VARIANT_CONFIG_DIR = REPO_ROOT / ".mise"
WORKFLOW_PATH = REPO_ROOT / ".github" / "workflows" / "ci.yml"

    "# scripts/generate_ci_workflow.py. Do not edit by hand; run\n"
    "# `mise run ci:generate-workflow` and commit the result.\n"
)


class ManifestError(ValueError):
    """Raised when the CI target manifest is structurally invalid."""


def load_manifest(path: Path = MANIFEST_PATH) -> dict:
    """Parse the CI target manifest, returning a plain dict."""
    with path.open("rb") as handle:
        return tomllib.load(handle)


def discover_variant_configs(variant_dir: Path = VARIANT_CONFIG_DIR) -> list[str]:
    """Return variant names backed by a ``.mise/config.<variant>.toml`` file."""
    names = []
    for config in sorted(variant_dir.glob("config.*.toml")):
        # config.<variant>.toml -> <variant>
        stem = config.name[len("config.") : -len(".toml")]
        names.append(stem)
    return names

part.text:
The code looks clean. One issue I notice: `validate` calls `resolve_envs(step, all_envs)` at line 129, which itself re-checks unknown variants (lines 68-73 in resolve_envs). This is now somewhat redundant since validate pre-checks at 122-128. But it's a harmless backstop and resolve_envs is also called standalone by `_render_step`. The redundancy is acceptable (defense in depth). Actually, since validate already validates unknown envs before calling resolve_envs, resolve_envs's own check won't trigger in validate. And in `_render_step` (render path, post-validation), envs are already valid. So resolve_envs's check is purely defensive. Fine. Keep it.

Now, there's a subtle thing: `validate` validates the manifest but `resolve_envs` is called both in validate AND in _render_step. That's fine.

Let me also reconsider: the `_render_step` uses `set(envs) != set(all_envs)` to decide whether to emit `if`. For the configure step (envs="all" → all_envs), set equal → no `if`. Good. For all others, subset → emit `if`. But what if a step lists ALL envs explicitly (not "all")? Then set equal → no `if` emitted, behaves like "all". That's correct behavior. Good.

Now let me verify the final full ci.yml once more and the round-trip is clean, then commit.

Let me also double-check: the manifest `section` field — validate doesn't check it, but that's fine (it's optional rendering hint). ruff/toml fine.

Let me run a final comprehensive verification:
1. Regenerate ci.yml.
2. git diff ci.yml should show only the generated changes vs the ORIGINAL committed ci.yml (the hand-written one). Wait — the committed ci.yml in git is still the ORIGINAL hand-written one (I haven't committed). The working tree ci.yml is the generated one. So `git diff ci.yml` shows original→generated. That's expected and correct.

But the round-trip test `test_generate_round_trips_committed_workflow` compares generated output to `gen.WORKFLOW_PATH.read_text()` — which is the WORKING TREE ci.yml (the generated one I wrote). So it compares generated-vs-generated → passes. But this test would ALSO pass if I hadn't written the file yet... no, generate() writes to a temp path, and compares to the committed file path (working tree). Since I ran generate() which wrote the working tree ci.yml, the working tree IS the generated output. So the test passes. 

But the REAL enforcement (ci:check-workflow) does: regenerate (overwrites working tree ci.yml) + `git diff --exit-code -- ci.yml` (compares working tree to HEAD). HEAD ci.yml is still the original hand-written one. So ci:check-workflow would show a DIFF (original→generated) and FAIL right now, because I haven't committed the generated ci.yml yet!

Right — that's expected. The generated ci.yml replaces the hand-written one, and committing it makes HEAD match. After commit, ci:check-workflow passes. Before commit, it shows the intended change. This is correct — the whole point is the generated file IS the committed file going forward.

So for my verification, I should confirm:
- generate() output == working tree ci.yml (round-trip) ✓ (test passes)
- After I commit, HEAD ci.yml == generated, so ci:check-workflow passes.

Let me verify the round-trip is clean (working tree == generated) and that the test confirms it. The test already does. Good.

Now, let me make sure I haven't left the working ci.yml in a stale state. Let me regenerate once more to be sure it's the final form, then review the complete diff, then commit.

Let me also reconsider: should the generator be executable (chmod +x)? It has a shebang. The existing `.mise/tasks/*` scripts are executable. `scripts/run-with-timeout.ps1` is not (it's .ps1). My generator is invoked via `python scripts/...` so it doesn't need +x. But having the shebang and making it executable is nice for direct invocation. Let me chmod +x it for consistency with the shebang. Actually, not necessary since mise calls `python scripts/...`. I'll leave it without +x to match run-with-timeout (non-executable script in scripts/). Hmm, but the shebang suggests it's meant to be runnable directly. Either way works. I'll make it executable since it has a shebang and is a CLI tool. Actually, let me check if other .py in repo are executable... there are none. I'll leave it as-is (not executable); `python scripts/generate_ci_workflow.py` is the documented invocation. Fine.

Let me do final verification and commit.

Actually, wait — let me reconsider the `ci:test-generator` and `ci:check-workflow` ordering within ci:lint once more, and whether `mise run` nested calls work. mise tasks calling `mise run <othertask>` via shell — this works but spawns a nested mise process. It's slightly heavyweight but fine. An alternative is to use mise's `depends` on ci:lint itself, but ci:lint needs ordered shell steps. The nested `mise run` is acceptable and clear.

However, there's a potential issue: `ci:check-workflow` has `depends = ["ci:generate-workflow"]`. When called via `mise run ci:check-workflow` from within ci:lint's shell, mise resolves the dependency and runs generate then the diff. Good. But does the nested `mise run` inherit the right cwd/env? It runs in the same repo (mise auto-detects config). The generate task writes to the absolute WORKFLOW_PATH. The diff uses relative `.github/workflows/ci.yml` from cwd. ci:lint runs at repo root (config_root). Nested mise run inherits cwd=repo root. So `git diff -- .github/workflows/ci.yml` works. Good.

Let me finalize. Let me regenerate ci.yml one final time, show the complete diff summary, and commit.

Let me also verify the manifest parses and the final test run, then commit with a good message.

# CI target support policy.
#
# Source of truth for the `variants` job in .github/workflows/ci.yml: which build
# variants run in CI, which GitHub runner each uses, and which mise task each
# variant runs. The workflow is generated from this file by

#

#

# `.mise/config.<variant>.toml` file; the generator cross-checks the two sets so
# neither can drift unnoticed.
#
# Steps appear in workflow display order. Each step field means:
#   name    step name shown in the Actions log (omit for an unnamed `run` step)
#   task    mise task id; the step runs `mise run <task>`
#   envs    variants the step runs on, as an explicit allow-list, or "all"
#   reason  optional rationale lines, rendered as workflow comments

display.text:
# CI target support policy.
#
# Source of truth for the `variants` job in .github/workflows/ci.yml: which build
# variants run in CI, which GitHub runner each uses, and which mise task each
# variant runs. The workflow is generated from this file by
# scripts/generate_ci_workflow.py:
#
#   mise run ci:generate-workflow
#
# CI fails (via `mise run ci:check-workflow`) if the checked-in workflow drifts
# from this manifest. Variant identifiers must each match a
# `.mise/config.<variant>.toml` file; the generator cross-checks the two sets so
# neither can drift unnoticed.
#
# Steps appear in workflow display order. Each step field means:
#   name    step name shown in the Actions log (omit for an unnamed `run` step)
#   task    mise task id; the step runs `mise run <task>`
#   envs    variants the step runs on, as an explicit allow-list, or "all"
#   reason  optional rationale lines, rendered as workflow comments

# GitHub Actions runner selected for each build variant.
[runners]
linux-arm64-egl = "ubuntu-24.04-arm"
linux-arm64-vulkan = "ubuntu-24.04-arm"
linux-x64-egl = "ubuntu-latest"
linux-x64-vulkan = "ubuntu-latest"
macos-arm64-metal = "macos-latest"
macos-arm64-vulkan = "macos-latest"
macos-arm64-egl = "macos-latest"
ios-arm64-metal = "macos-latest"
ios-simulator-arm64-metal = "macos-latest"
windows-x64-vulkan = "windows-2022"
windows-x64-wgl = "windows-2022"

# Native library: iOS variants cross-compile (build only); everything else tests.
[[steps]]
task = "configure"
envs = "all"

part.text:
Now let me verify the generated ci.yml is valid GitHub Actions YAML one more time (actionlint-style concerns), and check the YAML parses. I already verified it parses with PyYAML. 

Let me also double-check there are no issues with the `fromJson` expressions — actionlint is happy with this pattern (it's a standard documented pattern). 

Before committing, let me reconsider one thing: the generated ci.yml has very long `if:` lines. actionlint doesn't care about line length. But let me make sure the YAML scalars are valid. I parsed with PyYAML successfully, so they're valid YAML. Good.

Now, I'm concerned about whether `hk fix` (actionlint) will pass on the generated ci.yml. actionlint might warn about something. The original ci.yml passed actionlint (it's committed). My generated version uses `contains(fromJson('["..."]'), matrix.mise_env)` which is a valid, common pattern. actionlint should accept it. One potential actionlint concern: it might check that matrix values referenced exist, but `matrix.mise_env` is defined in the include. Good.

Let me also reconsider: actionlint might flag the `fromJson` with a warning about expression? No, it's standard.

I think we're good. Let me also verify the generated YAML doesn't have trailing whitespace or other issues that actionlint/yaml might dislike. PyYAML parsed it, so structurally fine.

Now, one more important verification: let me make sure the behavior is TRULY preserved by manually checking a couple of the trickier conditions against the original, beyond the automated test. The automated test already does this exhaustively for all 15 steps × 11 envs. I'm confident.

Let me now reconsider whether I've addressed all parts of the task:
1. ✅ "CI target support policy trapped in hand-written workflow" → now in declarative manifest.
2. ✅ "Make it declarative" → `.github/ci-targets.toml`.
3. ✅ "generate the checked-in workflow from manifests" → `scripts/generate_ci_workflow.py` reads manifest + variant configs, emits ci.yml.
4. ✅ "enforce that it stays in sync" → `ci:check-workflow` (regenerate + diff) in ci:lint; also validates runners==configs.
5. ✅ "Preserve current CI behavior unless clearly duplicated policy" → behavior-preservation test proves identical allow-lists; no behavior removed (the duplicated policy — env list in both CI and config files — is now cross-validated rather than removed).
6. ✅ "Keep normal contributor workflow simple" → contributors who don't touch CI targets see no change; manifest editors run one `mise run ci:generate-workflow`.
7. ✅ "test the generator" → 19 unit tests including behavior preservation, validation, determinism, round-trip.
8. "commit the finished change" → about to do.

Wait — "clearly duplicated policy." Is there duplicated policy I should remove? The task says preserve behavior "unless it is clearly duplicated policy." Let me reconsider: is there any actual duplication in the original that I'm carrying forward unnecessarily?

Looking at the original conditions, several steps had IDENTICAL conditions:
- Java FFM, Java JNI, .NET, Rust, Rust example, Zig example all used `!= macos-arm64-egl && !ios-`. That's 6 steps with the same condition. This is "duplicated policy" in the sense that the same predicate is written 6 times. But they're genuinely separate steps (different tasks) that happen to support the same env set. In the manifest, I list the envs explicitly per step (still 6 separate lists). Is that "duplication" I should factor out?

Hmm. I could introduce a named "support set" that multiple steps reference, e.g.:
```toml
[support_sets]
non_ios_non_angle = [...]

[[steps]]
envs = "$non_ios_non_angle"
```
But that adds complexity. The task says preserve behavior unless CLEARLY duplicated policy. The repeated env lists are duplicated DATA but each step legitimately owns its support set (they could diverge). Factoring them into a shared set would couple them — if one binding later needs to add/remove a variant, it'd have to leave the shared set. Explicit per-step lists are MORE correct (each step independently declares its support). So this isn't "duplicated policy to remove" — it's correctly independent declarations that happen to coincide today.

Actually, wait — is it "clearly duplicated policy"? The 6 identical conditions suggest there might be an underlying "tier" concept (e.g., "tier 1: fully supported bindings"). But the repo doesn't model it that way, and the conditions could legitimately diverge. I'll keep explicit per-step lists — that's the declarative, drift-resistant choice and preserves the ability for each to diverge. The repetition is acceptable and arguably desirable here.

So I won't factor out shared sets. This is a defensible reading: the "duplication" in the original was the env↔runner matrix being maintained in two places (ci.yml AND implicitly the config files), which I've now made consistent via cross-validation. That's the real duplication, addressed.

Actually, let me reconsider if I should call this out / ask the user. The task says "You may use ask_user for consequential architecture or scope questions." Whether to factor shared support-sets is a minor scope question, not consequential. I'll proceed without asking and note my reasoning in the commit/summary.

I'm confident in the approach. Let me commit.

Commit message: follow repo style. The repo has one commit "benchmark base". Let me write a concise conventional-style message. The repo uses mise tasks etc. Let me check if there's a commit convention. Only one commit exists ("benchmark base"), no clear convention. I'll write a clear, descriptive message.

Let me stage all the files and commit. I should NOT commit if formatters would change files — but I've verified line lengths and style. There's residual risk on the manifest TOML (dprint) and Python (ruff) formatting that I can't run. But I've matched conventions carefully. 

Actually, let me reconsider the risk once more. The `ci:lint` in CI runs `hk fix --all` then `git diff --exit-code`. If my committed files aren't format-stable, CI fails. The highest risk is the manifest TOML (taplo) since I reasoned about boundaries. Let me re-verify the manifest's inline arrays are all safely under 80 and multiline ones over 80, one more time, precisely. And reconsider trailing comma behavior.

Inline arrays in manifest:
1. `envs = ["ios-arm64-metal", "ios-simulator-arm64-metal"]` — line 43. Length: count `envs = ["ios-arm64-metal", "ios-simulator-arm64-metal"]` = 7+1+17+2+27+1 = 55. Under 80. taplo keeps inline. ✓ (matches pixi.toml inline style)
2. `envs = ["macos-arm64-metal", "macos-arm64-vulkan"]` (Swift binding) — 7+1+19+2+19+1 = 49. ✓
3. `envs = ["macos-arm64-metal"]` (Swift example) — 7+1+19+1 = 28. ✓

Multiline arrays: all the rest (8-item, 9-item, 4-item LWJGL=90chars inline, 4-item Kotlin). All >80 inline → taplo keeps multiline. ✓ And they match the `.mise/config.windows-*.toml` multiline style (2-space indent, trailing comma). ✓

`reason` arrays: 2 long strings → multiline. ✓

`runners`: simple `key = "value"` lines. ✓

The header comments: long lines (e.g., line 3 "Source of truth for the `variants` job in .github/workflows/ci.yml: which build" ~80 chars). taplo preserves comments as-is (doesn't reflow). The existing mise.toml has very long comment lines (e.g., the windows_default_file_shell_args comment is ~140 chars) and it's committed/stable, proving taplo doesn't reflow comments. ✓

So the manifest should be dprint-stable. 

Python files: all lines ≤88, 4-space indent, double quotes, trailing commas. ruff format should be a no-op. The one residual concern: ruff format's handling of the implicit string concatenation in `validate` (lines 126-127):
```python
                raise ManifestError(
                    f"{location} ({task}) lists unknown variants:"
                    f" {', '.join(unknown)}"
                )
```
ruff format does NOT merge implicitly-concatenated f-strings (it preserves them). And each line is ≤88. So stable. ✓

Also the `render()` list has implicit string concat entries (concurrency, required if). ruff format preserves. ✓

I'm confident. Let me also make the generator script executable? It has a shebang. Not required. Leave as-is.

One more: let me verify there are no tabs or CRLF in my files (dprint/ruff assume LF, 2/4 spaces). Let me check.

Actually, let me just commit. But first, let me reconsider whether to run `mise run fix` — I can't (no mise). The formatters will run in the user's pre-commit hook anyway, but MY commit is what matters for CI. Since I can't run them, I've hand-verified. If there's a tiny format nit, the user's pre-commit (hk, installed via mise) would fix-and-stage before they commit... but I'm committing directly. 

Given I can't run formatters, the safest path: commit now; the files are carefully authored to be format-stable. If CI reveals a nit, it'd be a trivial formatter-only follow-up. But I want to get it right the first time. I've done thorough verification.

input.command:
git commit -m "Generate CI workflow from declarative target manifest

The variants job's target support policy (env->runner map and which mise task
runs on which variants) was hand-written in .github/workflows/ci.yml and kept
drifting via fragile startsWith/!= conditions.

Move it to a declarative manifest (.github/ci-targets.toml) and generate the
checked-in workflow from it. The generator cross-checks the manifest against the
.mise/config.<variant>.toml files so a variant cannot exist in CI without a
config (or vice versa).

- .github/ci-targets.toml: source of truth for variants, runners, and the
  per-component support allow-lists.
- scripts/generate_ci_workflow.py: validates the manifest + variant configs and
  renders .github/workflows/ci.yml (pure stdlib).
- scripts/test_generate_ci_workflow.py: unit tests; the headline test proves
  each generated allow-list is identical to the original hand-written
  conditions across all variants, so behavior is preserved.
- mise.toml: ci:generate-workflow (regenerate), ci:check-workflow and
  ci:test-generator (enforced from ci:lint so the workflow cannot drift).
- dprint.jsonc: exclude the generated workflow; actionlint still validates it."

part.text:
Committed as `396cb83`. Summary of what changed:

**Declarative policy** — `.github/ci-targets.toml` is now the source of truth: the env→runner map plus, per component, an explicit allow-list of variants (replacing the fragile `startsWith`/`!=` compound conditions). Exclusion rationale (Kotlin/Native) lives in the manifest as `reason` and is rendered into the workflow as comments.

**Generator** — `scripts/generate_ci_workflow.py` (pure stdlib) validates the manifest and renders `ci.yml`. It cross-checks the manifest's variant set against the `.mise/config.<variant>.toml` files, so a variant can't exist in CI without a config or vice versa — that was the real duplication, now enforced rather than manually maintained.

**Enforcement** — `mise run ci:check-workflow` (regenerate + `git diff`) and `ci:test-generator` run from `ci:lint`, so the workflow can't drift. The generated `ci.yml` is excluded from dprint (generator owns its bytes); actionlint still validates it.

**Behavior preserved** — the headline test encodes every original condition as a predicate and asserts each generated allow-list selects the identical variant set across all 11 targets. 19 tests pass. Normal contributors who don't touch CI targets see no workflow change; manifest editors run one task: `mise run ci:generate-workflow`.

One note: I couldn't run `mise run fix`/ruff/dprint here (no network/tools in this env), so I authored files to be formatter-stable by matching existing repo conventions (verified inline/multiline TOML array boundaries against `pixi.toml`/`.mise` configs, all Python lines ≤88). Worth a quick `mise run fix` on your end to confirm, but they should be no-ops.
